coredata.dc:title: pH-responsive copper-cluster-based dual-emission ratiometric fluorescent probe for imaging of bacterial metabolism
coredata.dc:creator: Song, Shanliang
Zhao, Yue
Li, Yang
Yang, Xudong
Wang, Dong
Wen, Zhuoqi
Yang, Minghui
Lin, Quan
coredata.dc:description: The profiling of bacterial metabolism is of great significance in practical applications. Therefore, the development of ultrasensitive and highly selective probe for bacterial metabolism detection and imaging is extremely desirable. Herein, a novel dual-emission pH-response bacterial metabolism detection and imaging probe is successfully developed. This probe consists of large-sized and easily separated SiO2 microspheres, copper nanoclusters (Cu NCs) with red emission, and carbon dots (CDs) with blue emission through in-situ self-assembly. In this system, the fluorescence of Cu NCs is sensitive to pH change due to their obvious aggregation-induced emission enhancement (AIEE) property, while the blue fluorescence of CDs remained almost stable. Therefore, red fluorescence and blue fluorescence are compounded with different fluorescence intensity at different pH values, and their fluorescence ratio is also different. By observation of composite fluorescence color, the visual colorimetric pH detection can be realized with the change of pH value of 0.2 units. Utilizing this system, we are able to detect bacterial metabolism with high signal-to-noise ratio, and it can also be used for bacterial metabolic imaging. Therefore, the pH-responsive Cu NCs-based dual-emission ratiometric fluorescent probe we constructed can provide new ideas for bacterial detection, antimicrobial sterilization, and biological imaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ratiometric fluorescent probe
Copper nanoclusters
Aggregation induced emission enhancement
Imaging of bacterial metabolism

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of Cu NCs and dual-emission Cu NCs–SiO2@CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Emission spectrum of CDs in aqueous solution (1 mg/mL); inset: the photographs of CDs under visible light and UV light (365 nm) illumination. (b) Fluorescence values of CDs at different pH values. (c) Emission spectrum of Cu NCs in aqueous solution at pH = 5.0 (2 mg/mL); inset: the photographs of Cu NCs under visible light and UV light (365 nm) illumination. (d) The fluorescence spectra of Cu NCs at different pH values.

(a) A typical TEM image of as-prepared SiO2@CDs. The TEM elemental mapping of SiO2@CDs for silicon (b), oxygen (c), and carbon (d) elements. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Emission spectrum of as-prepared Cu NCs–SiO2@CDs, inset: the photographs of Cu NCs under visible light and UV light (365 nm) illumination. (b) The SEM image of as-prepared Cu NCs–SiO2@CDs.

(a) TEM image of as-prepared Cu NCs–SiO2@CDs, the TEM elemental mapping of Cu NCs–SiO2@CDs for silicon (b), oxygen (c), and copper (d) elements. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a) The fluorescence intensity spectra (excitation at 380 nm) of Cu NCs–SiO2@CDs in buffer solution with different pH value from 4.2 to 7.2; (b) The relationship between ratiometric fluorescence intensity (I643/I456) and pH value, inset: the corresponding fitting curve; (c) CIE chromaticity diagram showing the (x,y) color coordinates of Cu NCs–SiO2@CDs varying with pH value; (d) The photographs of Cu NCs–SiO2@CDs in buffer solution at different pH value under 365 nm UV light illumination. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Detection of bacterial metabolism by Cu NCs–SiO2@CDs at different times 0 h, pH = 7.2 (a); 1 h pH = 6.7 (b); and 3 h pH = 5.4 (c); a1, b1, and c1 are fluorescent photos of the bacterial metabolic system; a2, b2, and c2 reflect the metabolic amount of bacteria; c1, c2, and c3 are laser confocal imaging of bacterial metabolic system.
Preparation of dual-emission ratiometric Cu NCs–SiO2@CDs nanocomposite material for pH sensing.

head.ce:title: pH-responsive copper-cluster-based dual-emission ratiometric fluorescent probe for imaging of bacterial metabolism

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Center for Reproductive Medicine, Center for Prenatal Diagnosis, First Hospital, Jilin University, Changchun 130021, Jilin, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The profiling of bacterial metabolism is of great significance in practical applications. Therefore, the development of ultrasensitive and highly selective probe for bacterial metabolism detection and imaging is extremely desirable. Herein, a novel dual-emission pH-response bacterial metabolism detection and imaging probe is successfully developed. This probe consists of large-sized and easily separated SiO2 microspheres, copper nanoclusters (Cu NCs) with red emission, and carbon dots (CDs) with blue emission through in-situ self-assembly. In this system, the fluorescence of Cu NCs is sensitive to pH change due to their obvious aggregation-induced emission enhancement (AIEE) property, while the blue fluorescence of CDs remained almost stable. Therefore, red fluorescence and blue fluorescence are compounded with different fluorescence intensity at different pH values, and their fluorescence ratio is also different. By observation of composite fluorescence color, the visual colorimetric pH detection can be realized with the change of pH value of 0.2 units. Utilizing this system, we are able to detect bacterial metabolism with high signal-to-noise ratio, and it can also be used for bacterial metabolic imaging. Therefore, the pH-responsive Cu NCs-based dual-emission ratiometric fluorescent probe we constructed can provide new ideas for bacterial detection, antimicrobial sterilization, and biological imaging.

ce:section.ce:para: Bacteria are ubiquitous in our daily lives. Although there are some bacteria that are beneficial to mankind, such as lactobacillus and steptomyces, most bacteria do harm more than benefits, especially in terms of food storage, life, and health [1]. More than 20% of food is being wasted due to spoilage all over the world, which not only results in food waste and economic losses, but also causes environmental pollution. At the same time, the proliferation of various bacteria can cause a variety of diseases. For example, escherichia coli (E. coli), is one of the most common clinical pathogens that can cause a series of life-threatening diseases (e.g., sepsis, hemolytic uremic syndrome) and a huge medical and financial burden [2,3]. In order to minimize the problems caused by bacteria, early detection of bacterial reproduction and metabolism has become a very critical step. It is well known bacterial metabolism produces a large amount of lactic acid and carbonic acid, which can decreases the pH of the environment around growing bacteria. Therefore, the growth and metabolism of bacteria can be detected and imaged by monitoring the pH changing of the surrounding environment of bacterial [4]. Fluorescent probes are widely used in pH detection due to their advantages of high selectivity, sensitivity, convenience, and rapid operation [5,6]. Conventional fluorescent probes mainly include organic dyes, semiconductor quantum dots (QDs), and metal nanoclusters (NCs) [7–11]. Considering the toxicity of organic dyes and semiconductor quantum dots, fluorescent metal nanoclusters have attracted extensive attention as pH fluorescent probes.

Metal nanoclusters (NCs) consist of tens to hundreds of atoms and have a size close to 1–2 nm [12]. Due to their ultra-small size close to Fermi level electrons, they have unique optical and physical properties, such as strong fluorescence effect, low toxicity, good biocompatibility, simple preparation method and so on [13–16]. These advantages make them widely used in ion detection, biosensing, medical imaging, catalysis, and other fields [17–19]. Among them, copper nanoclusters (Cu NCs) have attracted great attention due to their unique advantages over other precious metals [20–24]. Especially, Cu NCs have special aggregation-induced emission enhancement (AIEE) properties [25]. Under certain conditions, such as solvent, ion, pH, and special substances, Cu NCs will aggregate to exhibit strong fluorescence [26,27]. This optical property is widely used by researchers. For example, Wang's group has designed a strongly fluorescent Cu NCs with AIEE properties as a sensitive detection probe for pH and H2O2 [28]. Feng Hui et al. designed a novel Cu NCs fluorescent probe that can be used to detect β-Galactosidase [29]. However, most of existing probes based on Cu NCs are ubiquitous in water system, so they are not easy to recycle leading to reagent waste and environmental pollution. It is urgent to prepare easily separated Cu NCs-based probes. What's more, most of traditional fluorescence detection platform are based on single emission, which means that many factors, including probe concentration, instrument sensitivity, and environmental condition, will have negative effects on detection results [30–34]. In contrast, dual-emission fluorescent materials show two different emission peaks under single excitation, which means that the built-in correction factor can be used as a colorimetric fluorescent probe to calibrate errors and avoid data distortion [35–41]. Therefore, the development of a easily separated Cu NCs-based probe with dual-emission fluorescence is of great significance for detecting bacterial metabolism.

Here, an easily separated Cu NCs-based probe with dual-emission ratiometric fluorescence was generated by in situ self-assembly. The synthetic processes are illustrated in Scheme 1 . Firstly, for easier separation, silicon nanospheres incorporated with blue fluorescent CDs was synthesized by Stöber method (namely SiO2@CDs) [42]. Then red fluorescent Cu NCs were assembled on the SiO2@CDs nanospheres by electrostatic interaction abbreviated Cu NCs–SiO2@CDs. Due to the obvious AIEE property of Cu NCs, dual-emission Cu NCs–SiO2@CDs can be used to detect the change of pH value by colorimetry. When the pH value increases gradually from 4.0 to 7.2, Cu NCs disperse gradually and their corresponding red emission intensity decreases gradually. At the same time, the blue fluorescence of CDs remains almost unchanged. Therefore, the composite fluorescence shows a color change from red to blue, which changes visually with the change of pH value of 0.2 units. This detection process is very simple and convenient. Subsequently, we applied this dual-emission probe to detect bacterial metabolism, which can quickly detect the pH value of bacterial metabolic system indirectly reflecting the bacterial reproduction. Besides, this dual-emission Cu NCs–SiO2@CDs can image bacteria with high signal-to-noise ratio. Therefore, the developed dual-emission materials can provide new ideas for bacterial detection, antimicrobial sterilization, and biological imaging.

We first synthesized fluorescent CDs through a hydrothermal method according to the reported method [7]. The morphology and structure of the fluorescent CDs were characterized by using a transmission electron microscope (TEM). As depicted in Fig. S1, the nearly spherical CDs have uniform size and good dispersibility. The size distribution was measured by counting 100 particles, and the average particle size of CDs was about 1.6 ± 0.2 nm (inset in Fig. S1). In the fluorescence spectra, (Fig. 1 a), it can be seen that the synthesized CDs have intense blue fluorescence at 450 nm (Fig. 1a) with excitation at 338 nm (Fig. S2). The aqueous solution of CDs has a narrow absorption peak at about 338 nm (Fig. S3). The obtained CDs exhibited a strong blue fluorescence under a 365 nm excitation light and a pale yellow color under visible light (inset in Fig. 1a). The fluorescence intensity of CDs has a strong stability. Even if the pH changes from 4.0 to 7.0, the fluorescence intensity of CDs does not change significantly (Fig. 1b). Next, pH-sensitive Cu NCs were successfully synthesized based on the reported methods [28]. As shown in Fig. 1c, the strong emission of Cu NCs was found at around 643 nm, and the solution of Cu NCs exhibited a red color under a hand-held UV lamp (inset in Fig. 1c). When the pH of prepared Cu NCs was changed, a pH-dependent fluorescence behavior was observed. As displayed in Fig. 1d, the fluorescent intensity of Cu NCs decreased when the pH increased from 4.0 to 7.2. Moreover, it is observed that the transparency of the solution increased obviously when the pH value increased from 4.0 to 7.2. Considering the AIEE property of Cu NCs, we speculated that the pH-sensitive response is due to the influence of pH on the aggregation state of Cu NCs. The pH variations lead to either aggregation or dispersion of the Cu NCs, which is accompanied by dramatic changes of the fluorescence intensity. In order to demonstrate such response mechanism, two samples with different pH values (pH = 7.0 and pH = 4.0) were collected and their morphologies were observed by TEM. As shown in Fig. S4, Cu NCs are relatively dispersed at pH = 7.0. In contrast, Cu NCs are obviously aggregated at pH = 4.0 showing a stronger fluorescence in fluorescence spectra (Fig. 1d). These results are consistent with our proposed mechanism. Subsequently, we explored the reversibility of pH detection by Cu NCs (Fig. S5). The results show that the fluorescence of Cu NCs decreased gradually with the number of cycles, which may be due to the poor stability of Cu NCs. Based on such AIEE properties, we can construct a dual-emission pH sensor probe using the response of Cu NCs to pH.

It is acknowledged that silicon nanospheres have light stability, good transparency, outstanding biocompatibility, high loading capacity, and low background signal properties [43,44]. Thus, we encapsulated CDs into SiO2 nanospheres in situ by Stöber method (namely SiO2@CDs). The SEM image of SiO2@CDs nanospheres is displayed in Fig. S6. The nanospheres exhibit good monodispersity with an average size of 700 nm. The large size of nanospheres makes them easy to separate from the system, also represents a convenient method for subsequent material recovery and protection detection platform. Particularly, the synthesized CDs were designed to be incorporated into SiO2 nanospheres, and the blue fluorescence of CDs was not affected. It can be seen that SiO2@CDs nanospheres solution emitted blue fluorescence at 450 nm (Fig. S7). Photographs of materials also show bright blue fluorescence under a 365 nm excitation light and light yellow under visible light (inset in Fig. S7). The spherical morphology of SiO2@CDs was characterized by TEM, as depicted in Fig. 2 a. To further prove that CDs was successfully encapsulated into SiO2 nanospheres, TEM-Mapping test was carried out. The results show that the synthesized SiO2@CDs is composed of silicon (Fig. 2b), oxygen (Fig. 2c) and carbon (Fig. 2d) elements, which are evenly distributed in the SiO2 nanospheres.

Previous reports have demonstrated that there are abundant hydroxyl groups on the surface of SiO2 spheres [42], and Cu NCs with penicillamine as stabilizer have many carboxyl and amino groups [28]. On this basis, SiO2@CDs with the surface modification of Cu NCs was synthesized by electrostatic interaction, abbreviated as Cu NCs–SiO2@CDs. A series of characterizations were used to investigate properties of Cu NCs–SiO2@CDs. The fluorescence spectrum of Cu NCs–SiO2@CDs is displayed in Fig. 3 a. It revealed that the Cu NCs–SiO2@CDs nanospheres exhibit two emission peaks at 456 and 643 nm, respectively, as a result of the incorporated CDs and Cu NCs. The strong red fluorescence was observed from Cu NCs–SiO2@CDs under 365 nm excitation light (inset in Fig. 3a), which demonstrates SiO2 nanospheres have good light penetration. The morphology and structure of the as-synthesized Cu NCs–SiO2@CDs were studied by using a SEM and TEM. The SEM (Fig. 3b) and TEM images (Fig. 4 a) showed spherical Cu NCs–SiO2@CDs with uniform size and excellent dispersibility. TEM-Mapping was further used to investigate the constituent elements of Cu NCs–SiO2@CDs. The results revealed that the synthesized Cu NCs–SiO2@CDs are composed of silicon (Fig. 4b), oxygen (Fig. 4c), and copper (Fig. 4d) elements. It can be seen that the copper element is evenly dispersed on the surface of the SiO2 nanospheres. Thus, we successfully constructed a dual-emission probe based on CDs and pH sensitive Cu NCs.

With two fluorescent materials separately modified in SiO2 nanospheres, the Cu NCs–SiO2@CDs shows a strong pH response. Fig. 5 a displayed the fluorescence intensity spectra of the dual-emission nanospheres in aqueous solution upon increasing the pH from 4.0 to 7.2. The emission bands at 456 and 643 nm can be assigned to the CDs and Cu NCs, respectively. The fluorescence intensity at 456 nm is stable and hardly changes in the range of pH from 4.0 to 7.2. In contrast, the fluorescence intensity at 643 nm decreases gradually as the pH changes from 4.0 to 7.2. Therefore, the ratio of the emission bands at 456 and 643 nm is able to sensitively respond to pH variations. Careful analysis showed that the colorimetric fluorescence of the two emission wavelengths (I643/I456) exhibited a linear relationship (Fig. 5b, y = −0.50x+3.83, R2 = 0.99). A CIE (Commission Internationale del’Eclairage 1931) diagram showed that the ratiometric fluorescence color of the dual-emission nanospheres changed with the value of pH (Fig. 5c). With the change of pH from 4.0 to 7.2, the composite fluorescence color of the material changes from red to blue. Analogously to the above results, the fluorescence photographs (Fig. 5d) under different pH conditions also prove that the dual-emission probe can provide a sensitive fluorescence response following small pH changes. More importantly, this material can be recovered by centrifugation, washing with neutral PBS, and vacuum drying for future use.

Based on the above response property, the difference of luminescence emitted by Cu NCs–SiO2@CDs nanospheres at different pH can be developed for visualization of bacterial growth. First of all, Cu NCs–SiO2@CDs nanospheres are cultured with E. coli in 24-well plates at different times. The small change of pH value (7.2, 6.7, and 5.4) can be visualized by fluorescent photographs under a 365 nm excitation light. The pH-sensitive Cu NCs–SiO2@CDs nanospheres in E. coli culture medium initially displayed a homogeneous blue emission color (Fig. 6 a1). After 1 h of growth and metabolism by E. coli, it can be observed that the fluorescence color of the nanospheres gradually changed from blue to light pink (Fig. 6b2). With further increased the incubation time to 3 h, the emission color of Cu NCs–SiO2@CDs nanospheres changed to red (Fig. 6c1). The bacterial growth and metabolism process were also monitored by confocal laser scanning microscopy. It can be seen that the overall color of the bacteria gradually changed from blue to red with the increase of metabolic time (Fig. 6a3-c3). These variations of fluorescence color of nanospheres are due to the substantial change in the local pH value. As the incubation time increased, a larger concentration of protons are produced during the growth and metabolism of E. coli, and the quantity of bacteria increased with the prolongation of culture time (Fig. 6a2-c2). Under the acidification conditions, the fluorescence intensity of Cu NCs on dual-emission system is intensely strengthened, and the red luminescence of Cu NCs is becoming the predominant color. Therefore, Cu NCs–SiO2@CDs nanospheres exhibit excellent fluorescence imaging in vitro of bacterial metabolic microenvironment. This process is very convenient and sensitive in an ambient environment. In addition, the change of the fluorescence intensity of Cu NCs–SiO2@CDs nanospheres in the neutral PBS buffer solution over time has also been tested. It can be seen from the Fig. S8 that there is no obvious change in the fluorescence of Cu NCs–SiO2@CDs nanospheres over time to 7 days, which proves that the material has good stability. The dual-emission nanospheres have many distinct features, such as low cost, non-toxicity, and ease to separate, and most importantly, they can improve the contrast and the signal-to-noise ratio.

All chemical reagents are directly commercially used without further purification. Citric acid, ethylenediamine, tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS), and d-penicillamine (DPA) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Cu(NO3)2, ethanol (CH3CH2OH), ammonia (NH3·H2O), hydrochloric acid (HCl), and sodium hydroxide were purchased from Aladdin company. Deionized (DI) water was used to prepare all aqueous solutions.

Fluorescence spectra were measured by Horiba JobinYvon Fluoromax 4C-L (France) spectrophotometer. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were taken by TECNA F20 transmission electron microscopy at an acceleration voltage of 200 kV. Composition of the composites was measured by an energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS) connected to a TEM instrument. Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) images were obtained using Hitachi S4800 + EDS. Confocal microscopy images were taken using Olympus Fluoview FV1000.

The synthesis of carbon dots (CDs) was based on previous report method with minor modifications as following: firstly, citric acid (1.0507 g) and ethylenediamine (335 μL) were dissolved in DI-water (10 mL), stirred until they were mixed uniformly, and then transferred into a Teflon reaction vessel and heated at 200 °C for 5 h [7]. After that, the reactor was naturally cooled to room temperature. The CDs powders were obtained by dialysis and freeze-drying. Then the purified CDs powders were dispersed in water to prepare the CDs solution with the concentration o 10 mg/mL and stored at 4 °C for further use.

The SiO2@CDs was prepared by a simple method [41]. Firstly, the CDs (10 mL) and TEOS (1 mL) were added into a mixed solution of ammonia (28%, 12.5 mL) and ethanol (125 mL) stirring mechanically for 1 h. Then the mixed solution of TEOS (9 mL) and ethanol (9 mL) was added dropwise (One drop every 3 s) into the above system. After stirring for 24 h, the pH of solution was adjusted to 5 with 1 M HCl. By centrifugation and washing with water and ethanol (3000 rpm, 3 min), SiO2@CDs powders were obtained. Disperse it into a neutral PBS buffer to prepare a 20 mg/mL solution for cryopreservation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of Cu NCs and dual-emission Cu NCs–SiO2@CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of DPA-stabilized Cu NCs was as follows: Cu(NO3)2 aqueous solution (100 mM, 40 μL) was added into DPA aqueous solution (10 mM, 4 mL) under vigorous stirring for 10 min [28]. The white precipitate was obtained, which showed red fluorescence under UV light. The mixture was further stirred at room temperature (25 °C) for 90 min, then, SiO2@CDs solution (20 mg/mL, 1 mL) was added and stirred strictly for 1 h. The precipitate was then centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 10 min and collected. Afterwards, the product was washed three times, following by freezing and lyophilizing to obtain the pure Cu NCs–SiO2@CDs powder.

Nutrient agar medium (32 g) was added to deionized water (about 1000 mL) and stirred to dissolve. The water solution was then distributed to the flask and incubated at 121 °C for 15 min in a high-pressure sterilizer. The solution was then diluted 3.5 times to avoid its adverse effects on fluorescent materials. Escherichia coli was laid loosely in a 24-well plate and cultured in a 37 °C cell culture chamber for 24 h. The cultured Escherichia coli (108 cfu/mL, 0.3 mL) was added to the Cu NCs–SiO2@CDs solution (3 mg/mL, 0.2 mL) and cultured at 37 °C. The mixture solution was collected at different time points (0 h, 1 h, and 3 h). The pH value was measured by pH meter, and the imaging at different pH was observed by laser confocal microscopy.

In conclusion, a novel dual-emission colorimetric fluorescent Cu NCs–SiO2@CDs probe was prepared. This probe is composed of large-sized and easily separated SiO2 microspheres, Cu NCs with red emission, and CDs with blue emission through in-situ self-assembly. In this platform, the fluorescence of Cu NCs can response to pH values due to their obvious AIEE property, while the blue fluorescence of CDs remained almost stable. As a result, the Cu NCs–SiO2@CDs probe is combined with red fluorescence and blue fluorescence, which can visually detect the change of pH value by observing the composite fluorescence color. The Cu NCs–SiO2@CDs probe can detect bacterial metabolism with high signal-to-noise ratio, which will provide information of the pH changes of bacterial metabolic system and the amount of bacterial reproduction. At the same time, this probe can also be developed for bacterial metabolic imaging. This dual emission colorimetric fluorescence probe provides a new idea for the research of bacteriology. It has great prospects in the fields of biological and chemical sensors, imaging, and cancer diagnosis.

S. Song designed experiments, performed statistical analysis, and manuscript editing; S. Song and Y. Zhao carried out literature research and experiments; Y. Li performed bacteria studies; Z. Wen carried out TEM analysis; X. Yang, D. Wang, Q. Lin, and M. Yang revised the manuscript. All authors discussed experiments and results.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Nature Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 51861145311 and 21174048), Program of Jilin Provincial Department of Science & Technology, China (Grant No. 20180520010JH), and the Industrial Technology Research and Development Project of Jilin Province Development and Reform Commission China (Grant No. 2019C050-10). Dr. M. Yang would like to thank for the National “Thousand Youth Talents” program of China and Ningbo 3315 program.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the Supplementary data to this article:

Typical TEM image of the as-prepared CDs (Fig. S1). Abs spectrum of CDs in aqueous solution (Fig. S2). Ex spectrum of CDs in aqueous solution (Fig. S3). TEM images of Cu NCs with dispersed state at pH = 7 (a) and aggregate state at pH = 4 (b) (Fig. S4). Cu NCs reversibly detect pH from 7.0 to 4.0 (Fig. S5). The SEM photograph of SiO2@CDs (Fig. S6). Em spectra of SiO2@CDs in aqueous solution; inset: the photographs of SiO2@CDs under visible light and UV light (365 nm) illumination (Fig. S7).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.talanta.2020.121621.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A convenient purification method for silver nanoclusters and its applications in fluorescent pH sensors for bacterial monitoring
Biosens. Bioelectron.
